coredata.dc:title: FacileelectrolyticsynthesisofPtandcarbonquantumdotscoloadedmultiwallcarbonnanotubeashighlyefficientelectrocatalystforhydrogenevolutionandethanoloxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, He
Xue, Shoufeng
Zhang, Jingjuan
Zhao, Man
Ma, Jianchun
Chen, Shuai
Zheng, Zhanfeng
Jia, Jianfeng
Wu, Haishun
coredata.dc:description: Designing high-performance Pt-based catalysts with low catalysts cost is still a great challenge. Here, we demonstrate a facile electrolysis-solvothermal technique for making Pt and carbonquantumdots coloaded multiwall carbon nanotube (Pt-CQDs/MWCNT) catalyst with the assistant of electricfield. Despite of the ultralow Pt amount (0.81 wt%) for the optimized Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalyst, it exhibits ultrahigh electroactivity and durability toward hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR). The mass activities of the catalyst for HER and EOR can be achieved to 4889.7 mA/mg (-40 mV) and 4917 mA mg−1 Pt (forward peak), which are 8.1 times and 5.6 times more active than those of commercial Pt/C respectively. The characterization results reveal that Pt nanpparticles (Pt NPs) with intrinsic structure play the key role in excellent catalytic performance for HER and EOR. CQDs not only support abundant anchoring points for fixation and dispersion of Pt NPs, but also construct 3D conductive network with MWCNT supporter for improving the catalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pt nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Multiwall carbon nanotube
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Ethanol oxidation reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Activity of as-prepared Pt-CQDs/MWCNT for HER
Activity of as-prepared Pt-CQDs/MWCNT for EOR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process for Pt-CQDs/MWCNT.

(a) Representative TEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 10 V. Inset in panel (a) shows the nanoparticle core size histogram. (b) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 10 V. (c) Representative TEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 20 V. Inset in panel (c) shows the nanoparticle core size histogram. (d) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 20 V. (e) Representative TEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V. Inset in panel (e) shows the nanoparticle core size histogram. (f) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V. (g) HAADF-STEM image and (h-j) EDS maps of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) XPS survey spectra, (c) Pt 4f XPS spectra, and (d) Raman spectra of the samples prepared at different eletrolysis voltages.

(a) LSV (area activity) of the samples prepared at different eletrolysis voltages. (b) LSV (mass activity) of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V and Pt/C. (c) LSV curves of the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V before and after 2000 CV cycles in the stability test in 0.5 M H2SO4 for the HER. (d) Tafel curves, (e) The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) and (f) EIS curves of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V and the compared samples for the HER respectively.

Cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared samples in 1 M KOH (a) and 1 M KOH + 1 M ethanol (b) respectively at room temperature. (c) Pt mass activity for ethanol oxidation by four as-prepared catalysts. (d) Chronoamperometric curves of these four catalyst-modified electrodes for EOR.
The relationship between the Pt loading amount and HER activity or EOR activity for as-prepared samples at different electrolysis voltages.

(a) LSV and (b) Tafel curves of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalysts prepared with different electrolytes for the HER in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 at room temperature, respectively. (c) Cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared samples in 1 M KOH + 1 M ethanol at room temperature. (d) Chronoamperometric curves of these four catalyst-modified electrodes for EOR.

(a) XPS survey spectra and (b) UV–vis images of the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with different electrolytes. (c) Representative TEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBACF3SO3 electrolyte. Inset in panel (c) shows the nanoparticle core size histogram. (d) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBACF3SO3 electrolyte. (e) Representative TEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBAF electrolyte. Inset in panel (e) shows the nanoparticle core size histogram. (f) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBAF electrolyte. (g) Representative HRTEM micrographs of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBAOH electrolyte. (h) Raman survey spectra of the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with different electrolytes. (i) The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) and (j) EIS curves of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with different electrolytes respectively.
The relationship between the Pt loading amount and HER activity or DEFCs activity for as-prepared samples with different electrolytes.
Schematic illustration of the proposed reaction mechanism for HER reaction and EOR reaction over Pt-CQDs/MWCNT.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing high-performance Pt-based catalysts with low catalysts cost is still a great challenge. Here, we demonstrate a facile electrolysis-solvothermal technique for making Pt and carbonquantumdots coloaded multiwall carbon nanotube (Pt-CQDs/MWCNT) catalyst with the assistant of electricfield. Despite of the ultralow Pt amount (0.81 wt%) for the optimized Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalyst, it exhibits ultrahigh electroactivity and durability toward hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR). The mass activities of the catalyst for HER and EOR can be achieved to 4889.7 mA/mg (-40 mV) and 4917 mA mg−1 Pt (forward peak), which are 8.1 times and 5.6 times more active than those of commercial Pt/C respectively. The characterization results reveal that Pt nanpparticles (Pt NPs) with intrinsic structure play the key role in excellent catalytic performance for HER and EOR. CQDs not only support abundant anchoring points for fixation and dispersion of Pt NPs, but also construct 3D conductive network with MWCNT supporter for improving the catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical water-splitting is one of the most promising approaches to generate sustainable clean hydrogen energy for replacing fossil fuels in the future [1–9]. However, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), as the vital half-reaction for water splitting, is sluggish in the kinetic process. Direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFCs) draw worldwide attention in electro-chemical power generation used in portable electronic devices because of their high energy density, low pollutant emission and ease of handling [10–16]. While the slow reaction rate of ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) blocked the wide application of this technology. Thus efficient catalysts are needed to speed up these reactions. Pt-based materials have been proved as the most efficient HER and EOR catalysts, but the huge adoption of this noble material has been hindered by the high price and scare storage. Recently, Pt single-atom or Pt nanoparticles (NPs) loaded catalysts with ultralow Pt usage and high atom utilization efficiency have been reported to show exceptional catalytic properties, comparable with that of commercial 20% Pt/C [3,17-19]. This could be an effective strategy to solve the problem.

Carbon-based materials are commonly used as support to highly disperse Pt species [5,20-24]. Among them, carbon nanotube (CNT) attracts considerable attention owing to the large surface area, good electric conductivity and facile fabrication into three-dimensional framework [5,25-27]. However, pristine CNT is chemically inert and difficult for Pt nanoparticles to anchor. Improving the anchoring effect of CNT is mostly done by oxidation with HNO3 or H2SO4-HNO3 [28]. Although these generated oxygen groups on CNT can act as specific nucleation sites for highly dispersion of Pt clusters, the electric conductivity of CNT is limited by introduction of structural defect. The competitive ratio has not yet been achieved between electric conductivity and anchoring function. Recently, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were adopted to decorate on multiwall-carbon nanotube (MWCNT) [29] or reduced graphene oxide (RGO) to support Pt NPs. The existence of NCQDs not only can be served as anchoring point to disperse and stabilize Pt NPs on MWCNT, but perverse the good electric conductivity of CNT. However, the synthesis of Pt, CQDs-coloaded catalyst is relatively complicate, involving co-reduction of Pt compound precursor and pre-prepared CQDs. Facile synthesis of this composite for its wide application is challenging and demanding.

As far as the generation of CQDs is concerned, electrochemical carbonization of various bulk carbon-containing materials such as low-molecular-weight alcohols was already successfully applied to prepare CQDs [30]. Moreover, it has been proven that platinum anodic electrode can be dissolved in solution at voltages exceeding the Pt dissolution onset potential [5]. These research results inspired us to investigate whether dissolution of Pt and formation of CQDs can be achieved simultaneously by electrochemical method.

Herein, we design a facile method to synthesize highly efficient Pt, CQDs-coloaded MWCNT catalyst (Pt-CQDs/MWCNT) toward HER and EOR with the assistant of electricfield. During electrolysis, CQDs and Pt NPs were in-situ generated in the electrolyte simultaneously. Then both of the CQDs and Pt NPs were loaded on MWCNT in the subsequent solvothermal process. In this work, three problems have been solved: firstly, a facile method was designed to generate Pt nanoparticles and CQDs simultaneously. The obtained catalyst showed excellent electrochemical activity for both HER and EOR reactions. Secondly, the structure-activity relationship of as-prepared catalyst was revealed. The activity of the catalysts for HER and EOR was attributed to the highly dispersed ultrafine Pt nanoparticles and large electrochemically active surface area (ECSA). Finally, the loading amount and dispersion of Pt NPs could be regulated by electrolysis conditions such as electrolytic voltage and the type of supporting electrolyte. This work could play a significant guiding rule in facile synthesis of Pt, CQDs-coloaded composites.

Multiwall-carbon nanotube was purchased from Xianfeng Nano Company. Tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4, 98%, powder), Tetrabutylammonium borohydrlde (TBABH4, 95%, powder), Tetrabutylammonium fluoridetrihydrate (TBAF, 98%, solid), Tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (TBAOH, 98%, solid). Proprylene carbonate (PC, ≥99.9%) was purchased from Aladdin. N, N-dimethylformamide (99.9%), and propylene carbonate (99.9%) was got from Aladdin, Anhydrous ethanol (99.5%) purchased from Woke Company.

Two platinum wire electrodes were immersed in proprylene carbonate (PC) solvent with tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (defined as TBABF4; 0.1 M) serving as supporting electrolyte. A voltage of 30 V was applied for 12 h. Then multiwall-carbon nanotube (MWCNT) was put into above solution. After stirring for 12 h, the mixture was transferred into 100 ml Teflon autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the samples was collected by filtration and washed with PC for three times and ethanol for twice, then dried in a vacuum oven for 8 h at 60 °C. Finally, the samples were annealed at 400 °C in N2 atmosphere for 60 min to remove residue solvent. The obtained sample was defined as Pt-CQDs/MWCNT.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV-185) with Cu Ka radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a JobinYvon LabRAM HR800 micro-Raman spectrometer using a laser excitation of 532 nm at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Thermo Fisher SCIENTIFIC using Al Ka X- ray source. The morphologies of the samples were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F). The chemical composition (Pt) of the as-prepared samples was determined by an Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES) method.

All the electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai) with a standard three-electrode system. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) was used as the support for the working electrode. Immobilization of sample was achieved by drop-casting 5 ul sample (5 mg sample and 20 µL Nafion solution were dispersed in 1 ml water-ethanol solution with volume ratio of 3:1) onto GC working electrodes. As for HER, saturated Hg/Hg2Cl2 and a graphite rod were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively. During the HER activity test, Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted in the range of 100 to −500 mV (vs. RHE) at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. The stability measurements were performed by cyclic voltanmmetry scanning 2000 cycles (CV, sweep rate, 50 mV·s−1). The ethanol oxidation was carried out in 1 M KOH or 1 M KOH + 1 M ethanol solution in the potential range from 0.224 to 1.424 V (vs. RHE). Modified GC electrode was also used as working electrodes, and saturated Hg/HgO and a platinum plate were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively.

The two-step synthesis process of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalyst has been depicted in Fig. 1 : (1) Electrochemical in-situ generation of CQDs and Pt nanoparticles. Proprylene carbonate (PC) solvent and Pt wire electrode are served as the carbon and Pt sources respectively. (2) The composition of CQDs and Pt nanoparticles with MWCNT by direct solvothermal treatment. It is known that dissolution of Pt and formation of CQDs in the electrolyte solution can be regulated by electrolytic voltage. Therefore the influence of working voltage on the loading amount and size distribution of Pt NPs and CQDs are first investigated.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of these Pt-CQDs/MWCNT samples prepared under different working voltage are shown in Fig. 2 . We can see that the nanoparticles are dispersed uniformly on MWCNTs supporter for catalysts prepared under 10 V (Fig. 2a), 20 V (Fig. 2c) and 30 V (Fig. 2e), and the diameters of these nanoparticles mainly range from 1 nm to 5 nm (The average diameters of these obtained Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalysts are 2.2 nm (Fig. 2a), 2.0 nm (Fig. 2c) and 2.4 nm (Fig. 2e) for the electrolysis voltage at 10 V, 20 V and 30 V respectively. From the HRTEM, it can be seen that there are two kinds of particles. These dark dots with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (Fig. 2b), can be assigned to (111) crystal face of Pt [9,31], indicating the generation of Pt. While those dots with light color (Fig. 2f) show a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal face of graphitic carbon [32], meaning the formation of CQDs. The HADDF-STEM image (Fig. 2g) for Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V displays isolated bright dots attributable to heavy atoms on the MWCNT, also confirming the successful anchoring of Pt NPs. The elemental mapping images reveal the homogeneous spatial distributions of carbon (Fig. 2h), platinum (Fig. 2i) and oxygen (Fig. 2j). This result further indicates that Pt NPs generated over MWCNT. For catalyst prepared under 5 V (Fig. S1), no quantum dots or nanoparticles can be found on MWCNT, meaning no or seldom Pt nanoparticles and CQDs are generated under low voltage. SEM is also used to investigate the morphology of these obtained samples. As observed from SEM images (Fig. S2), all the samples obtained at different voltages show the similar morphology and typical carbon nanotube struture like MWCNT. It’s obviously seen that no large nanoparticles formed on the MWCNTs supporter. Duo to the limit of resolution, the nanoparticles over the MWCNT shown in TEM images can’t be observed from the SEM images.

Visually, the number of nanoparticles or quantum dots increase with the enhancement of working voltage. This is further verified by the UV–vis (Fig. S3) and ICP-OES results (Table S1). Previous UV–vis results shows that the electrolyte after electrolysis under 20 V or 30 V exhibits obvious fluorescence with a stronger absorption peak in the range of 250–300 nm than that via electrolysis under 5 V or 10 V, suggestion the formation of more CQDs at higher working voltage [9]. ICP-OES results also show the high loading amount of Pt over the samples prepraed at high working voltage: 5 V (0.0 wt%,), 10 V (0.26 wt%), 20 V (0.41 wt%) and 30 V (0.81 wt%). Above result verifies that the Pt and CQDs are loaded over the MWCNT, and the loading amonut increases as the electrolytic voltage goes up. The higher Pt and CQDs loading might lead to electrocatalytic properties.

XRD spectra (Fig. 3 a) of all as-prepared samples mainly showed a strong peak at 2θ = 26.5° which is assigned to the (002) reflection peak of graphite. However, no XRD reflections ascribed to Pt species can be found on these catalysts. This is understandable because of the very low content and high dispersion of Pt species on MWCNT [33]. XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3b) displays that Pt-CQDs/MWCNT samples prepared at different voltages are mostly composed of carbon, but some trace amount elements like Pt ans O also exist. As seen from the high-resolution Pt 4f spectra (Fig. 3c), except for the sample prepared at 5 V, the other samples all show two peaks located at around 71.7 and 75.0 eV, which are assigned to 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 of Pt0 respecrively [34]. This result demonstrates that the Pt NPs are generated at higher preparation voltage (10 V, 20 V and 30 V) and existed in the form of elemental state rather than ion state, consistent with previous TEM and HRTEM results.

Raman spectra of graphitic materials are used to identify the density of defects. The Raman spectra (Fig. 3d) of these samples exhibit three prominent peaks, including D band at about 1350 cm−1, G band at 1580 cm−1 and 2D band at 2650 cm−1. The ID/IG ratio (the intensities of the D (ID) band relative to the G(IG) band) is used to estimate the the density of Raman-sensitive defects [35]. It can be seen that the defects of the obtained Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalysts increase with the enhancement of preparation voltage: ID/IG (30 V, 0.46) > ID/IG (20 V, 0.40) > ID/IG (10 V, 0.39) > ID/IG (5 V, 0.38). The highest ID/IG ratio for sample prepared under 30 V can be attributed to the highest amount of Pt NPs and CQDs formed on MWCNT, which is benefitial for the electrochemical catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of as-prepared Pt-CQDs/MWCNT for HER
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance of the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT samples toward HER was firstly evaluated using a standard three-electrode system (without iR correction). Fig. 4 a displays the typical linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) curves recorded from different electrocatalysts. Obviously, Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared at 30 V (Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30) exhibites excellent HER catalytic activity among these prepared catalysts. Its overpotential at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2 is 35.3 mV, comparable with that of commercial Pt/C (27.8 mV), and much lower than those prepared at 20 V (46.9 mV), 10 V (50.8 mV), 5 V (274 mV) and MWCNT (257 mV). To rationally compare the catalytic performance of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT (30 V) with Pt/C, their mass activities (normalized to the current/Pt loading mass (A/mg)) for HER was studied (Fig. 4b). It can be seen that Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 V displays much higher HER catalytic activity than commercial Pt/C catalysts. The mass activities for Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 V is 4889.7 mA/mg at −40 mV, 8.1 times more active than that of Pt/C (600.8 mA/mg). The exceptional HER activity of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 V can be attributed to the high dispersity of ultrafine Pt over Pt-CQDs/MWCNT according to previous XRD, ICP-OES, TEM and HRTEM results. ICP-OES, XPS and TEM results also show that the loading amount of Pt increases with the increased electrolytic voltage, which explains the promoted HER activity accompanied by the increased electrolytic voltage (Pt NPs are the main active sites for HER). It should be noted that even for the sample Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 with the highest Pt amount, its content of Pt is only 0.81%. This untralow amount of Pt is comparable with that of single atom of Pt. But the HER acitvity of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 is much higher than that of some Pt single-atom loaded catalysts [3]. In addition, the LSV curve of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 remain almost unchanged before and after 2000 CV scanning (Fig. 4c), indicating its strong durability. What’s more, as shown in Fig. 4d, Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 shows a much smaller Tafel slope (34 mV·dec−1) than those samples prepared at 20 V (48 mV·dec−1), 10 V (57 mV·dec−1), 5 V (154 mV·dec−1) and MWCNT (142 mV·dec−1), revealing more favorable electrocatalytic kinetics on Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 for HER. The electrochemical active surface aera (ECSA) (Fig. 4e) determined by electrochemical double-layer capacitances (Cdl) results (Fig. S4) [36] suggest that the ECSA of the as-prepared samples enlarge with the increased electrolysis voltage, consistent with HER activity. This is concluded that enhanced Pt content and the high dispersion of Pt NPs over Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 contribute to its super ECSA. The largest electrochemical surface area and the most accessable active sites of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 are also the reason for its excellent HER area activity. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) result (Fig. 4f) of all the samples shows that as-prepared catalysts exhibits smaller charge transfer resistances and faster reaction rates as the elelctrolysis voltage goes up. The small charge transfer resistance of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 is originated from higher uploading amount of generated Pt NPs and CQDs over MWCNT, which is also benifical for improving its HER activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of as-prepared Pt-CQDs/MWCNT for EOR
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance of as-prepared samples for EOR was also investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometric techniques. Fig. 5 a and 5b displays the CV curves of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT samples prepared at different electrolytic voltages and commercial Pt/C electrodes in the potential range from 0.224 to 1.424 V in 1 M KOH and 1 M KOH + 1 M ethanol respectively. As observed, these CV curves (Fig. 5a) recorded in 1 M KOH are almost similar and don’t show obvious characteristic current peaks of ethanol oxidation. When adding ethanol into the KOH solution, each curve (Fig. 5b) exhibits two characteristic current peaks. The oxidation peak in forward scan is considered as one of the major parameters characterizing EOR activity. While for the sample prepared at 5 V (Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-5, no Pt loading), the CV curve (Fig. S5) tested in ethanol solution is almost the same with that tested in the solution without ethanol, suggesting its inactivity for EOR. This result further verifies that the Pt NPs are the active sites for EOR. Fig. 5c shows that the mass activity (MA) of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 at forward peak position (fp) is much better than those prepared at 20 V, 10 V or Pt/C. The statistical value of MA (fp) for Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 is 4917 mA mg−1 Pt, 5.8 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C (867.8 mA mg−1 Pt). The long-term stability of these catalysts was also evaluated by chronoamperometric techniques. As observed in Fig. 5d, Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 displays better EOR stability than other catalysts after 7200 s. The enhanced durability of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 may be originated from good dispersion of Pt NPs and CQDs over MWCNT.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the formation of Pt NPs and CQDs is influenced by the electrolytic voltage. It’s understandable that the electric current applied also increases with the enhancement of electrolytic voltage as seen from Table S2. At the electrolysis voltage of 5 V, neither Pt NPs nor CQDs generate. As the electrolysis voltage goes up to 10 V, trace amount of Pt NPs are produced. When the electrolysis voltage achieves to 20 V or 30 V, CQDs and more Pt NPs generate. As a result, the loading amount of Pt NPs and CQDs over MWCNT increase with the enhanced electrolytic voltage. The probable reaction mechanism for HER reaction and EOR reaction over Pt-CQDs/MWCNT is proposed as shown in Scheme 1 . The characterization like TEM, XRD etc. suggest that the ultrafine Pt NPs highly disperse on MWCNT. Because the Pt NPs with superior orbital struture act as the active sites for HER and EOR [3,21,25,37-40], the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 with the highest Pt content (0.81 wt%) among these samples shows the best catalytic performance for HER and EOR, and is comparable with the previously reported catalysts (Tables S3 and S4) [5,9,22,24,41-46]. As for CQDs, as abundant nucleating and anchoring points, they could improve the fixation and dispersion of Pt NPs, and promote the charge transfer rate of the catalyst by constructing a 3D conductive network with MWCNT via π-π bonding [44,45].

Since Pt nanoparticles and CQDs are generated in the electrolysis process, Pt originated from the dissolution of Pt electrode and CQDs comes from the polymerization of solvent, it is not clear whether the supporting electrolyte participate in the formation. To this end, three other supporting electrolytes with different anions (TBACF3SO3 TBAF, TBAOH) are chosen to investigate their impact on the electrocatalytic properties. Fig. 7 a exhibits the HER activity of these as-prepared samples. It can be seen that Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 shows excellent HER activity among these obtained catalysts. Its overpotential at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2 is 35.3 mV, superior to that of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBACF3SO3 (39.5 mV), TBAF (45.6 mV), TBAOH (296 mV) and pure MWCNT (257 mV). What’s more, the Tafel slope (Fig. 7b) of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 (34 mV·dec−1) is smaller than that prepared at TBACF3SO3 (39 mV·dec−1), TBAF (46 mV·dec−1) and TBAOH (150 mV·dec−1), revealing more favorable electrocatalytic kinetics for HER. The solvent effect on the HER activity is also studied. The commonly used solvents including dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and N-Methylpyrrolidinone (NMP) are applied to compare with propylene carbonate (PC). From the LSV results (Fig. S6), it can be seen that Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-30 prepared with PC solvent exhibits an overpotential of 35.3 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2, which is smaller than those prepared with DMF (43.1 mV), DMSO (114.2 mV) and NMP (117.0 mV). This result indicates that the solvent indeed affects the HER activity and the obtained catalyst prepared using PC exhibits superior activity to that using other solvents. EOR performances of these samples prepared with different electrolyte were also investigated. Obviously observed in Fig. 7c, the MA (fp) of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT prepared with TBABF4 is much better than that prepared with TBACF3SO3 and TBAF. And the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 also shows outstanding EOR long-term stability in comparison with other samples after 7200 s (Fig. 7d). But for the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-OH without loading of Pt, its CV curve (Fig. S7) tested in the ethanol solution is nearly the same to that tested in the KOH solution, suggesting hardly any catalytic behavior for EOR.

To uncover the intrinsic differences of these catalysts made from different supporting electrolyte, the following work has been done. (1) ICP-OES results (Table S5) show the type of supporting electrolyte affects the loading amount of Pt on MWCNT. The Pt loading amount are listed as below: TBABF4 (0.81 wt%,), TBACF3SO3 (0.73 wt%), TBAF (0.12 wt%) and TBAOH (0.0 wt%). The XPS result (Fig. 8 a) further proves above ICP-OES results that Pt is formed over these samples prepared with TBABF4, TBACF3SO3 and TBAF eletrolyte rather than TBAOH eletrolyte. This is in accordance with their electrocatalytic activities, suggesting that Pt is the main active sites for HER and EOR. (2) Among these four different electrolyte solutions, only TBABF4 or TBACF3SO3 electrolyte solution after electrolysis exhibits obvious fluorescence with a strong absorption peak in the range of 250–300 nm (Fig. 8b), implying the mass formation of CQDs. Especially in TBACF3SO3 electrolyte solution, the CQDs concentration is higher than in TBABF4 solution, meaning more CQDs generatated over Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 sample. (3) TEM images of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 (Fig. 8c and d) and Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-F (Fig. 8e and f) shows similar morphologies with that of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4. While the sample prepared with TBAOH electrolyte didn’t show any nanoparticles or quantum dots (Fig. 8g), suggesting no Pt NPs or CQDs generated on this sample. This explains the poor electrocatalytic activities of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-OH. (4) From Raman spectra (Fig. 8h), we can see that Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 or Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 shows higher ID/IG ratio than Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-F or Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-OH due to the mass formation of Pt NPs and CQDs which contributes to the defects. Meanwile, the ID/IG ratio of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 is higher than that of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 perhaps beacuse of the higher amount of CQDs. (5) Inferred from electrochemical double-layer capacitances (Cdl) results (Fig. S8), the ECSA (Fig. 8i) of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 is higher than that of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-F, Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-OH and MWCNT, but lower than that of Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3. The EIS result (Fig. 8j) shows Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 and Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 with Pt NPs and CQDs catalysts exhibits smaller charge transfer resistances than Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-F and Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-OH, suggesting faster reaction rates. While Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-CF3SO3 shows higher reaction rate than Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 probably due to its larger CQDs uploading. As seen from above, despite of more CQDs, Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-TBACF3SO3 show slightly lower electrocatalytic activities than Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 due to its relatively lower amount of highly dispersed Pt NPs.

Above result demonstrates that the formation and amount of Pt NPs and CQDs can also be ajusted through the type of supporting electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 9 . Despite of the same preparation voltage of 30 V, the current values (Table S6) were different: I (TBAOH) < I (TBAF) < I (TBABF4) ≈ I (TBACF3SO3). Therefore, the contents of Pt NPs and CQDs over the obtained catalysts are different. The TBAOH electrolyte shows inferior ionization and low electric current, thus neither Pt nor CQDs is generated. While in the TBAF electrolyte solution, only trace amount of Pt and CQDs are generated in the electrolysis process and loaded on MWCNT in the subsequent solvent thermal process. In the TBABF4 or TBACF3SO3 electrolyte solution, more carbon quantum dots and Pt NPs was generated and loaded on MWCNT. The HER and EOR activity are positively related to the Pt loading amount. Thus the Pt-CQDs/MWCNT-BF4 with the most highly dispersed Pt NPs shows the best HER and EOR activity among these samples.

In summary, we established a facile electrolysis and solvothermal method to synthesis Pt and CQDs coloaded MWCNT catalyst. During first electrolysis process, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Pt nanoparticles (NPs) are in-situ prepared simultaneously. Moreover, the loading amount of Pt NPs over MWCNT increases with the enhanced electrolysis voltage. Despite of the same electrolysis voltage, the contents of Pt NPs and CQDs over the obtained catalysts prepared with different electrolyte are obviously different. The HER and EOR mass activities of the optimized Pt-CQDs/MWCNT catalyst are 8.1 times (−40 mV) and 5.6 times (fp) more active than the commercial Pt/C respectively. The structure-activity relationship suggests that the excellent performance of the obtained Pt-CQDs/MWCNT is attributed to the the synergetic effect between generated Pt NPs and CQDs over MWCNT. Pt NPs with intrinsic structure take the key role in excellent catalytic performance for HER and EOR. CQDs as anchoring points boost the fixation and dispersion of Pt NPs. Moreover, combining with MWCNT supporter via π-π bonding, CQDs constructs 3D conductive network and improve the whole catalytic performance. The present work provides new insights into the simultaneous synthesis of Pt NPs and CQDs electrocatalysts with assistant of electric field, simplifying the entire preparation processes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21571119), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-12-1035), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211393, 201901D211398), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institution in Shanxi (2019L0466), the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion (J19-20-605), Shanxi Normal University School Fund (ZR1707) and 1331 Engineering of Shanxi Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127271.
